era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Amenhotep II. The New Kingdom is at its territorial peak. In Anatolia, the Hittite Empire is consolidating under Tudhaliya III. In the Aegean, Mycenaean palace culture is flourishing in Late Helladic IIIA. In Mesopotamia, Kassite Babylon dominates. In China, the Shang Dynasty is in its middle period (Anyang phase).
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources for this period are problematic; oracle bone inscriptions exist but are difficult to date precisely)

events.title: Shang Dynasty Oracle Bone Divination Practices
events.description: During the Shang Dynasty's Anyang phase, oracle bone divination was a central practice of royal governance. Kings consulted oracle bones (scapulae and plastrons) to make decisions about warfare, agriculture, and ritual. These bones, inscribed with divination records, provide evidence of Shang administrative and religious practices. The practice reflects the integration of divination into state decision-making.
key_figures: Shang kings (names uncertain for this specific period)
events.certainty_note: Oracle bones are dated archaeologically to the Anyang phase (c. 1250–1050 BCE in conventional chronology), but precise annual dating is not possible. This entry is assigned to this period by convention.

events.title: Mycenaean Palace Culture in Late Helladic IIIA
events.description: Mycenaean palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos were at their height during Late Helladic IIIA (c. 1425–1390 BCE). These palatial centers controlled regional trade networks, maintained military forces, and produced distinctive pottery and luxury goods. The palaces were administrative, religious, and economic centers that dominated the Aegean.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos excavations

events.certainty_note: Late Helladic IIIA is well-dated archaeologically through pottery typology and stratigraphic sequences. The period is conventionally dated c. 1425–1390 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Shang Dynasty and oracle bone inscriptions is highly contested. Some scholars place the Anyang phase earlier (c. 1300–1050 BCE), while others argue for later dates. The conventional chronology used here (c. 1250–1050 BCE) is not universally accepted. Additionally, the interpretation of oracle bones as evidence of 'divination' versus 'record-keeping' is debated among scholars.
historiographic_note: For this year, documentation is extremely sparse and geographically fragmented. Egyptian and Hittite sources dominate the historical record, while Chinese and Aegean evidence is archaeological rather than textual. The dating of events in China and the Aegean relies on archaeological typology and stratigraphy, which are less precise than textual chronologies. No sources exist for most of the world during this period.

graph_edges.note: Mycenaean palace culture continued to develop through Late Helladic IIIA and IIIB
